onboarding.welcomeDesc: Your personal training ground for FAANG interviews. Practice patterns, track your progress, and level up your skills.
onboarding.pickTracks: What do you want to practice?
onboarding.pickTracksDesc: Pick the tracks you want to focus on. You can change this anytime.
onboarding.readyDesc: Start with a lesson to learn the pattern, then solve problems to build muscle memory. De aquí pa'l FAANG.

output.idle: Run or Submit to see results

problem.solveFirst: Solve the problem first, or reveal if you're stuck.

capitalOne.journeySub: Four stages from application to offer. Each stage filters hard — show up ready.

journey.what: Initial screen with a technical recruiter. High-level: background, role interest, salary expectations, timeline. Non-technical.
journey.evaluated: Culture fit at a surface level, motivation, whether your background maps to an open req.

journey.what: Timed CodeSignal screen. Four problems of increasing weight (100 / 200 / 300 / 400). You run tests locally; only the final submit counts.
journey.evaluated: Correctness, pattern recognition, and pacing. Published pass threshold is ~600; aim for 800+ for a safe signal.

journey.what: Four back-to-back rounds (~45 min each) with breaks: Coding, Coding + Job Fit, System Design OR Behavioral, Business Case.
journey.evaluated: Technical depth, communication, structured reasoning, and fit against the 4 Capital One values across every round.

journey.what: Debrief happens same-week. If you pass, recruiter returns with a verbal offer, then a written packet.
journey.evaluated: Nothing technical. At this stage it's compensation negotiation and start-date alignment.

capitalOne.distinctiveSub: Prep that works for FAANG won't cover this. These are the lenses Capital One interviewers use.

distinctive.title: A bank, not a social network
distinctive.body: Capital One regulates itself like a bank because it is one. Every system design must answer: is this PCI-in-scope? What's the audit trail? How do we prove we did the right thing to a regulator? Interviewers probe for this reflex.

distinctive.title: Money demands strong consistency
distinctive.body: You cannot eventually-consistency your way through a ledger. Expect probing on transactions, idempotency, exactly-once processing, and when strong consistency is worth the latency cost. Saying 'Cassandra' for a transfer pipeline is an instant red flag.

distinctive.title: The 4 values are the behavioral spine
distinctive.body: Excellence, Do The Right Thing, Respect for Individuals, Succeed Together. Every behavioral prompt maps to one of these. Rehearsing STAR stories with each value in mind is non-negotiable prep.

distinctive.title: JVM-heavy enterprise stack
distinctive.body: Java is the dominant backend language; AWS is the cloud. Microservices, Kafka for eventing, Postgres/Aurora, observability via Datadog. You won't be tested on brand names, but familiarity with the shape helps you speak their dialect.

capitalOne.planSub: Three tracks. Pick one — come back for the others. The mocks are where real preparation happens.

plan.title: Mock Power Day
plan.subtitle: 3-hour virtual onsite simulator
plan.body: The full 4-round loop end-to-end: two coding rounds, a system-design-or-behavioral coin flip, and a business case. Rubrics live, judge feedback at the end. Run this twice before your real Power Day.

plan.title: Mock GCA
plan.subtitle: 70-minute CodeSignal-style screen
plan.body: Four problems sampled from the GCA pool weighted 100/200/300/400. Timed. All-or-nothing scoring. 10 curated scenarios from warmup to boss-mix.

plan.title: Random Practice
plan.subtitle: One problem at a time, untimed
plan.body: Pull a random code exercise from Capital One's pool. Solve, run, next. Great for warming up before a mock or filling 20-minute slots during the week.

topic.title: Power Day — Capital One Virtual Onsite
topic.blurb: Seven chapters that prep you for the 3-hour, 4-round virtual onsite. Read in order or jump to what you need.

timeline.title: 01 · The day at a glance
timeline.blurb: A clock-by-clock view of all four rounds and the breaks between them.

concepts.title: 02 · Key concepts
concepts.blurb: The seven terms that unlock the rest of the overview — tradeoffs, STAR, frameworks, and more.

rounds.title: 03 · The 4 rounds
rounds.blurb: Per-round deep-dive: what they evaluate, common pitfalls, and how to pace each one.

values.title: 04 · The 4 values
values.blurb: How Capital One actually probes Excellence, DTRT, Respect for Individuals, and Succeed Together.

glossary.title: 05 · Enterprise architecture glossary
glossary.blurb: Category-before-product for sys-design rounds. Twelve categories of infrastructure decoded.

antiPatterns.title: 06 · Anti-patterns
antiPatterns.blurb: The four mistakes that cost otherwise strong candidates a loop, and what to say instead.

prep.title: 07 · Preparation timeline
prep.blurb: A week-before / day-before / hour-before / during-the-day schedule.

topic.title: GCA — General Coding Assessment
topic.blurb: Five chapters on how to crack the 70-minute CodeSignal screen Capital One uses to filter coding rounds.

scoring.title: 01 · How scoring actually works
scoring.blurb: Max 1000. All-or-nothing per problem. Hidden tests grade. Know this before you start.

modules.title: 02 · The 4 modules
modules.blurb: Module-by-module: what's tested, the representative pattern, pacing, and the #1 trap.

patterns.title: 03 · Pattern fingerprints
patterns.blurb: Ten algorithmic patterns to recognize on sight. The fingerprint is what lets you pick the approach in 90 seconds.

skip.title: 04 · The skip-and-return rule
skip.blurb: When a problem stalls, the pacing move that saves your score — and how to come back to it.

antiPatterns.title: 05 · Anti-patterns
antiPatterns.blurb: Coding-before-naming, premature optimization, and ignoring constraints. The three silent killers.

gca.title: GCA · 70 minutes, 4 problems, 1000 points
gca.summary: A timed coding screen Capital One uses early in the loop. Four problems of increasing weight. All-or-nothing scoring per problem.

scoring.title: Max score: 1000
scoring.body: Weights add up: 100 + 200 + 300 + 400. Capital One's published threshold is ~600, but stretch for 800+.

scoring.title: All-or-nothing per problem
scoring.body: Pass all tests = full weight. One hidden edge-case fail = zero. Correctness is king.

scoring.title: Hidden tests do the grading
scoring.body: Sample tests are a sanity check. The grader hammers with large inputs and nasty edges.

scoring.title: One submission, no penalty for waiting
scoring.body: You can run your code as many times as you want. Only the last submit at timeout is graded.

gca.modulesHeader: The 4 modules — what's tested, how to attack

modulesDeep.title: Module 1 — Warmup
modulesDeep.body: Arrays, strings, basic loops. Worth 100. Pass this fast to save time.
modulesDeep.pattern: Usually a simple linear scan or character-by-character transform.
modulesDeep.pacing: ~8 min. If you're at 12 and still stuck, something is off — reread the statement.
modulesDeep.trap: Off-by-one on string indices. Always sanity-check with length 0 and 1.

modulesDeep.title: Module 2 — Data Manipulation
modulesDeep.body: Hashmaps, sorting, prefix sums, sliding windows. Worth 200.
modulesDeep.pattern: Hashmap for dedup/count, or sort + two pointers.
modulesDeep.pacing: ~15 min. Have the fingerprint in your head before you type.
modulesDeep.trap: O(n²) inner loops passing samples but timing out on graders.

modulesDeep.title: Module 3 — Implementation
modulesDeep.body: Grid BFS/DFS, state machines, event replay, stack/queue patterns. Worth 300.
modulesDeep.pattern: 2D grid as implicit graph, or monotonic stack, or event-fold over a stream.
modulesDeep.pacing: ~20 min. Draw the example on paper first — grid bugs are the #1 time sink.
modulesDeep.trap: Visited set mutated inside recursion without a reset. Forgot direction vectors for diagonal cases.

modulesDeep.title: Module 4 — Algorithm Patterns
modulesDeep.body: Binary search, greedy, graph shortest-path, 1D DP. Worth 400.
modulesDeep.pattern: Binary-search-on-answer, greedy-by-end, Dijkstra, or DP[i] = f(DP[i-1]).
modulesDeep.pacing: ~25 min. Spend the first 3 minutes identifying the pattern — don't write a line until you've named it.
modulesDeep.trap: Greedy that's locally right but globally wrong. DP recurrence missing the base case.

fingerprints.signal: Sorted array, pair-sum, or in-place compaction
Shortest path in an unweighted grid / min-steps
Minimize max or maximize min under a monotonic check
Next greater / smaller element, largest rectangle

skip: Set a per-module budget BEFORE you start coding.
If you're 5 min past the budget with no working idea, skip. The clock is your enemy, not your ego.
Write a one-line TODO of your best guess before skipping — you'll pick up faster on return.
Come back to skipped problems in reverse order (hardest remaining first) once easier ones pass.

antiPatterns.why: You end up writing O(n²) when O(n log n) was required, and debug for 15 minutes.
antiPatterns.fix: Spend 90 seconds identifying the fingerprint. Say it out loud in your head: 'This is sliding window.'

antiPatterns.why: You can't optimize what you haven't made correct. Brute force that passes samples gives partial confidence.
antiPatterns.fix: Correct brute force → test → optimize. Only skip brute force if the input size makes it impossible.

antiPatterns.why: n ≤ 10^5 vs n ≤ 10^3 is the difference between O(n log n) required and O(n²) acceptable.
antiPatterns.fix: Read constraints FIRST. They tell you which pattern is eligible.

gca.tip2: If a problem stalls > 5 min past its budget, skip and return.
gca.tip3: Read all 4 statements at minute zero so the subconscious can cook.
gca.tip4: Correctness > speed. Partial passes score zero — verify edges.

powerDay.title: Power Day · 3 hours, 4 rounds
powerDay.summary: The full onsite loop. Four ~45-minute rounds back-to-back. The mix varies but the rubric is fixed: technical depth, communication, and fit against the 4 Capital One values.

roundsDeep.title: Round 1 — Coding
roundsDeep.body: Live DS/Algo problem on a shared editor. They watch you think out loud.
roundsDeep.evaluated: Correctness, edge-case reasoning, narration. They're scoring your process, not just the final code.
pitfalls: Going silent while coding — they can't evaluate what they can't hear.
Jumping to code before agreeing on the approach and complexity target.
Treating the interviewer's hint as an attack instead of a collaboration signal.
roundsDeep.pacing: 5 min clarify + agree on approach · 25 min code · 5 min test edge cases · 5 min complexity + improvement discussion.

roundsDeep.title: Round 2 — Coding + Job Fit
roundsDeep.body: A second coding problem, plus a conversation about why this role, why Capital One.
roundsDeep.evaluated: Technical breadth + genuine interest. They're watching for rehearsed answers vs specific, personal ones.
pitfalls: Generic 'I love innovation' answers — they've heard it 100 times this week.
Not connecting your trajectory to something concrete Capital One builds.
Spending so long on the behavioral half you rush the code half (or vice versa).
roundsDeep.pacing: 25 min coding · 20 min 'why Capital One' + career trajectory. Expect drill-down questions on specifics.

roundsDeep.title: Round 3 — System Design or Behavioral
roundsDeep.body: Coin-flip between banking sys-design (card auth, fraud, statements) and a deep behavioral drill.
roundsDeep.evaluated: For design: requirements → estimations → components → tradeoffs. For behavioral: STAR with numbers, values-aligned signal, no drift.
pitfalls: Sys-design: naming products (Kafka, Redis) without the category and reason. Skipping capacity math.
Behavioral: 'we' instead of 'I' when asked about your specific decision. Heroic framing on team wins.
Both: not anchoring to Capital One's banking context (money = strong consistency, audit = first-class).
roundsDeep.pacing: Sys-design: 5 min requirements · 5 min estimates · 20 min components · 15 min deepening on 1-2 areas they probe. Behavioral: 1-2 stories, 8-12 min each.

roundsDeep.title: Round 4 — Business Case
roundsDeep.body: Unit economics, frameworks, sensitivity. Structure first, compute second.
roundsDeep.evaluated: Structured thinking, quantitative reasoning, and the ability to DEFEND an assumption when pressed.
pitfalls: Computing numbers before naming a framework — looks unstructured.
Picking a framework and then abandoning it mid-analysis.
Over-precise numbers ($127.43) that signal false confidence. Use ranges.
roundsDeep.pacing: 5 min clarify + pick framework · 10 min size the problem · 15 min analysis · 10 min recommendation + sensitivity.

powerDay.valuesHeader: The 4 values — how Capital One actually probes

valuesDeep.title: Excellence
valuesDeep.body: Bar-raising, root-cause over patch, data-driven quality.
valuesDeep.signal: Names a specific bar you raised with numbers. Discusses quality tradeoffs that cost you short-term.
valuesDeep.antiSignal: Conflates working extra hours with raising the bar. Describes effort, not outcome.
valuesDeep.petPeeve: 'I spent weekends on it' without explaining what specifically got better and why.

valuesDeep.title: Do The Right Thing
valuesDeep.body: Holding the line under pressure, with a named cost.
valuesDeep.signal: Names the specific cost you paid (political, timeline, relationship). Verbatim language of what you said.
valuesDeep.antiSignal: Describes speaking up without quoting yourself. Makes the decision sound easy or inevitable.
valuesDeep.petPeeve: 'I reported it' with no tension — if there was no cost, there was no decision.

valuesDeep.title: Respect for Individuals
valuesDeep.body: Dignity under pressure, credit flow, bias-awareness.
valuesDeep.signal: Says what you chose NOT to say. Credit flows to less-visible contributors. Names a bias you caught in yourself.
valuesDeep.antiSignal: Uses 'convinced' as resolution — people were overpowered, not persuaded. Teammates are props for growth arc.
valuesDeep.petPeeve: Stories where you were 'right' about a teammate being 'wrong' — zero bias acknowledgment.

valuesDeep.title: Succeed Together
valuesDeep.body: Shared outcomes, proactive unblocking, no solo heroics.
valuesDeep.signal: Names teammates. Shows a capability that belongs to the team, not to you. Proactive unblocking signals.
valuesDeep.antiSignal: Team success with no named teammates. Martyr framing on drudge work.
valuesDeep.petPeeve: 'We shipped it' without saying who did what — generic team language.

powerDay.glossaryHeader: Enterprise architecture glossary — category first, product second
powerDay.glossarySub: If you only name the product, you look like you're cargo-culting. Name the category and the failure mode it solves; then the product comes naturally.

glossary.why: Decouples producer from consumer; buffers spikes; enables fan-out.
Sub-ms reads; hot-key shedding; session storage.
ACID transactions; strong consistency; money lives here.
Horizontal scale on a predictable key; eventual consistency OK.
Cheap durable blobs; logs, backups, static assets.
Emit row changes as events; drive downstream indexes/caches.
Full-text + aggregations at latency SQL can't hit.
Auth, rate-limit, routing at the edge.
Mutual TLS, retries, tracing, traffic shifting — out of your service.
Decouple deploy from release; targeted rollouts; kill switches.

antiPatterns.why: Naming the product without the reason signals cargo-cult. You need to show you understand WHY the category matters before naming a specific tool.
antiPatterns.fix: 'I need a messaging broker — something that decouples producer from consumer and buffers spikes. In practice that's Kafka or RabbitMQ; I'd reach for Kafka if I need replay or very high throughput.'

antiPatterns.why: In behavioral rounds, 'we' language masks your specific contribution. Capital One explicitly probes for individual ownership.
antiPatterns.fix: Say 'I did X, Y led Z, Z paired with me on W.' Name teammates. Make your slice crisp.

antiPatterns.why: False precision in a business case signals overconfidence in shaky assumptions. Interviewers will push on the weakest assumption.
antiPatterns.fix: 'Somewhere between $100K and $150K, most sensitive to customer-acquisition cost which I assumed at $45.' Own the range, name the sensitivity.

antiPatterns.why: Stopping at 'I don't know' costs signal. It's fine not to know — but what you do next is what they evaluate.
antiPatterns.fix: 'I haven't used that pattern specifically. My mental model would be X because Y. What am I missing?' Stay in dialogue.

tasks: Run 2 full Mock Power Days in Anabasis. One in the morning, one after lunch to simulate fatigue.
Re-read your resume and prepare 3 STAR stories per Capital One value.
Skim Capital One's product line — know what One, Eno, and their card rewards stack actually do.
One light Mock GCA (40 min) — just to stay warm, don't grind.
Review your STAR stories out loud. Time each one; target 8-12 min.
Sleep. Everything else is optimization around rest.
Open the editor you'll use. Warm fingers with one easy problem — don't start fresh cold.
Read your one-pager of STAR stories one last time. Close it.
Narrate every decision. If you're quiet, they can't score your thinking.
On 'I don't know': validate → mental model → what you'd reach for → what you'd need to verify.
Between rounds: stretch, hydrate, reset. Do NOT post-mortem the round you just finished — it poisons the next one.

powerDay.conceptsHeader: Key concepts · the vocabulary that unlocks the rest of this page
powerDay.conceptsSub: Everything below uses these terms. If any of them sound like jargon — read this section first. Each card explains what the concept is, why Capital One specifically evaluates it, and what it looks like in a real answer.

concepts.definition: A tradeoff is naming out loud the COST of a technical choice. 'I pick A, which means I give up B.' Not just 'A is better,' but 'A is better FOR X, and I'm accepting Y to get it.'
concepts.why: Capital One hires senior engineers to make judgment calls under ambiguity. If you never name a cost, you look like someone who thinks there's always a right answer — and that's a junior signal.
concepts.example: Weak: 'I'd use Postgres.' Strong: 'I'd pick Postgres over Cassandra here. I gain strong consistency for the ledger — every transfer is atomic. I pay by not scaling writes horizontally past ~50K/sec without heavy sharding; at our volume that's fine, but I'd revisit if we hit that ceiling.'

concepts.definition: A four-beat structure for answering behavioral questions. Situation (the context), Task (what you specifically had to do), Action (what you did — 'I', not 'we'), Result (what changed, with numbers).
concepts.why: Capital One behavioral interviewers are trained to extract STAR. If you ramble chronologically they have to pull it out of you — that loses signal. Delivering STAR cleanly means they can score faster and deeper.
concepts.example: '[S] Our payments team was hitting 4% transaction failures during holiday spikes. [T] I was tech lead; my job was to get us under 0.5% before Black Friday. [A] I proposed and shipped a circuit-breaker pattern in front of the downstream KYC service, then added a per-customer queue so retries didn't stampede. [R] Failures dropped to 0.2%, and we sustained 3× normal volume on Black Friday without paging.'

concepts.definition: Whenever your story contains something measurable — latency, errors, team size, dollar impact, percentage — say the number. If you don't know it, estimate and flag it as an estimate. Never replace a number with an adjective.
concepts.why: At a bank, everything maps to dollars or regulatory risk. Interviewers cannot evaluate 'much faster' — they can evaluate 'p99 went from 800 ms to 120 ms'. Precision is the signal.
concepts.example: Weak: 'We significantly reduced latency.' Strong: 'We cut p99 from 800 ms to 120 ms on the authorization path. About 2 million transactions per day, so at old latency ~3.2 minutes of aggregated user wait per second was just queued.'

concepts.definition: In system design, always name the architectural CATEGORY you need before naming a specific product. Say 'a messaging broker' before you say 'Kafka'. The category explains the role; the product is the brand you happen to know.
concepts.why: Naming Kafka without saying why reads as cargo-cult — like you're pattern-matching a résumé buzzword. Naming the category shows you understand the PROBLEM it solves, which is what they're actually testing.
concepts.example: Weak: 'I'd put Kafka between these services.' Strong: 'Between the payment processor and the fraud pipeline I need a messaging broker — something that decouples them so a slow fraud check doesn't back-pressure payments, and that buffers spikes. In practice I'd reach for Kafka here because I also want event replay.'

concepts.definition: In a case interview, a framework is an EXPLICIT structured lens you announce at the start and use to drive your analysis. Examples: Profit Tree (revenue - cost, broken into drivers), 3Cs (Company, Customer, Competition), 4Ps (Product, Price, Place, Promotion), Porter's Five Forces.
concepts.why: Without a framework, your analysis looks like guessing. Picking one in 30 seconds shows you have a structured approach to ambiguity — exactly what Capital One wants for someone who'll be making product + business decisions.
concepts.example: 'The question is why is the credit card business losing money. I'll use a profit tree: Revenue = interchange fees + interest + annual fees; Cost = fraud losses + rewards + acquisition + servicing. Let me walk through each and flag the biggest suspects.' That 20-second opening IS the framework — and you now have a roadmap for the next 15 minutes.

concepts.definition: After you pick a framework or a design, the interviewer will push on it: 'Why this framework?' 'Why not 3Cs?' 'What about X?' Defending means you have reasons ready — not defensiveness, but a confident 'here's why, and here's when I'd switch'.
concepts.why: Capital One wants people who can commit under uncertainty and still update. 'Defending' is the opposite of 'abandoning the framework when challenged'. If you flip every time pushed, you signal you never had conviction.
concepts.example: Interviewer: 'Why profit tree, not Porter's?' You: 'Porter's is better when the question is about market position or competitive dynamics. This one is specifically about a P&L that's turning negative, so starting from the P&L structure itself is more direct. If the root cause turns out to be competitive pricing pressure, I'd pivot to Porter's at that point.'

concepts.definition: When you hit something you haven't seen — a technology, a pattern, a business term — you do NOT stop at 'I don't know'. You say you don't know, then narrate your inference chain out loud using first principles.
concepts.why: Capital One interviewers have seen 'I don't know' kill otherwise strong loops. They don't expect you to know everything. They DO expect you to keep thinking visibly. Silence + confusion is the failure mode; narrated inference is the signal.
concepts.example: 'I haven't worked with CDC pipelines specifically. My mental model: if we need downstream systems to react to database changes in near-real-time, we need to get the change events out without double-writing from the app. That means tailing the database log somehow. I'd reach for something that reads the write-ahead log and publishes events — am I on the right track?'
